edges.description: Failed left-right organizer signaling disrupts conserved downstream laterality pathways that pattern asymmetric organ development.

nodes.description: Heterotaxy arises when motile cilia-dependent left-right organizer function fails during early embryogenesis. Defective cilium movement perturbs the earliest symmetry-breaking events and prevents robust specification of the left-right axis.

evidence.reference_title: Disorders of left-right asymmetry: heterotaxy and situs inversus.
evidence.snippet: Cilia function is critical to the development of proper organ laterality.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding laterality disorders and the left-right organizer: Insights from zebrafish.
evidence.snippet: Mutations identified in patients with laterality disorders implicate motile cilia in establishing LR asymmetry.

nodes.description: Downstream of cilia-driven symmetry breaking, abnormal nodal signaling and impaired PITX2 activation disrupt left-right pattern formation across the developing heart and abdominal viscera. This laterality-patterning failure yields the cardio-visceral organ discordance that defines visceral heterotaxy.

evidence.snippet: This pathway involves initial asymmetric activation of a nodal signaling cascade at the embryonic node, followed by its propagation to the left lateral plate mesoderm and activation of left-sided expression of the Pitx2 transcription factor specifying visceral organ asymmetry.
Analysis of model organisms and gene expression during early development suggests ZIC3-related heterotaxy occurs due to defects at the earliest stage of left-right axis formation.
